original_abstract: Lipopeptides represent a versatile class of amphiphilic molecules characterized by a peptide headgroup coupled to one or more lipid chains. This review examines the biophysical properties and diverse biomedical applications of both naturally occurring and synthetic lipopeptides. Two primary categories are analyzed: bacterially-derived cyclic lipopeptides, such as daptomycin and surfactin, which function as potent antimicrobial and antifungal agents; and linear lipopeptides, including Toll-like receptor agonists used in immunotherapy and bio-inspired sequences like Matrixyl utilized in cosmeceuticals. A central focus is placed on the self-assembly behavior of these molecules; depending on molecular architecture and environmental factors like pH and temperature, they spontaneously organize into various supramolecular nanostructures, ranging from spherical and wormlike micelles to extended bilayer nanotapes and fibrils. The article further explores current understanding regarding the interplay between these self-assembled morphologies and biological efficacy, highlighting how the high-density presentation of peptide motifs on nanostructured surfaces may influence their therapeutic performance and interaction with cell membranes.
humanized_abstract: Lipopeptides (LPs) are a broad class of amphiphilic compounds composed of a peptide headgroup covalently attached to one or more lipid tails. This review highlights the biophysical properties and biomedical applications of both naturally occurring and synthetic LPs. The first section addresses two main groups of bacterially derived cyclic LPs such as daptomycin and surfactin, which act as broad-spectrum antimicrobial and antifungal agents, and linear LPs like Toll-like receptor (TLR) agonists applied in immunotherapy, and bio-inspired sequences similar to that of the cosmeceutical ingredient Matrixyl. The self-assembly behavior of the LPs is analyzed in the second section in relation to their molecular structure and their response to changes in the solution environment such as pH and temperature. This is shown to affect their spontaneous self-assembly into nanostructures including, for example, liposomes, micelles (spherical and wormlike), nano-discs, and bilayer nanotapes and fibrils. In the third section, the complexity of the relationship between LP self-assembly and their function is highlighted. This includes a discussion of their increased biomedical efficacy resulting from their high density presentation on their nanostructured assemblies, and their possible interaction with cellular membranes.
